caveats: a: No six-digit wage-weighted task study was found.
a: BLS tutor data cover self-employed, public, and other private settings outside the lens.
a: Some exposed content and assessment tasks are already automated, so only the residual opportunity belongs in this primitive.
rho: The Stanford trial measured learning progress from tutor assistance, not labor removed or avoided in LMM firms.
rho: Implementation may increase service quality or tutor capacity without permitting equivalent payroll reduction.
rho: Professional-exam, special-needs, and K-12 tutoring have different quality and compliance constraints.
e: The 2022 NAICS index includes online learning marketplaces as well as direct providers, increasing business-model heterogeneity.
e: The frozen LMM firm count is itself an estimate and may include firms whose normalized EBITDA falls outside the target band.
e: Public listings and franchise examples are not representative samples of the full industry.
s5: No source measures a five-year transfer probability for eligible LMM 611691 firms.
s5: BizBuySell voluntarily reported sales skew small and combine childcare, schools, test preparation, and other education businesses.
s5: McKinsey's modeled retirement transitions include closures and firms across industries and size bands.
q: No direct study measures five-year pass-through of AI-driven tutoring savings.
q: Exam-prep courses, weekly remediation, professional review, and school contracts differ materially in price elasticity.
q: Volume displacement is excluded here and reflected in demand and operator-required quantities.
d5: Employment projections combine demand, productivity, and delivery-model shifts and cover ten rather than five years.
d5: SAT and ACT takers overlap, and school-day testing does not necessarily generate commercial preparation demand.
d5: NAEP academic need does not establish ability or willingness to pay for private tutoring.
o: The BLS statement is directional and does not quantify service-volume substitution.
o: The Stanford trial studied a Southern school district and math tutoring, not consumer LMM operators across subjects.
o: Rapid improvements in model reliability, multimodal interaction, and verified curriculum alignment could move this range downward.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Families and students commonly buy per-session, monthly, prepaid-package, membership, or fixed-course instruction; schools and organizations may buy cohorts or contracted tutoring. Revenue repeats through weekly sessions, multi-month programs, successive subjects, exam cycles, referrals, and institutional renewals.
lens.excluded_activities: Solo tutors without a transferable operating business, captive school or employer programs, nonprofit or government services without transferable control, educational testing without instruction, degree-granting schools, and software products that do not provide an accountable tutoring or preparation service.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market tutoring and exam-preparation firms that repeatedly sell academic remediation, enrichment, standardized-test preparation, professional-exam review, or related online or center-based instruction to students, families, schools, or employers.

a.bridge: BLS directly describes tutor duties and AI substitution but does not publish a wage-weighted, not-yet-automated task share for NAICS 611691. I map practice-question and lesson generation, routine explanations, feedback, progress summaries, scheduling, lead response, and basic assessment to exposed work, and retain motivation, diagnosis, relationship management, safeguarding, sales, and difficult misconceptions as less exposed. The mapping may overstate exposure where firms already use adaptive software and understate it where administration remains manual.
caveats: No six-digit wage-weighted task study was found.
BLS tutor data cover self-employed, public, and other private settings outside the lens.
Some exposed content and assessment tasks are already automated, so only the residual opportunity belongs in this primitive.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted activity census across 25 LMM tutoring firms, separating already-automated work from instructor delivery, preparation, assessment, parent reporting, sales, scheduling, and center management, followed by controlled AI time-and-quality trials.
a.rationale: Core tutoring work includes repeated review, drills, feedback, lesson preparation, and progress monitoring. BLS explicitly says generative AI and online software can complete many regular tutor activities and allow practice-test inventories to support services previously delivered only by personal tutors; a live-tutoring trial also shows AI can improve tutor guidance rather than only replace sessions.

d5.bridge: BLS projects tutor employment, not constant-price service quantity; College Board and ACT measure test participation, Common App measures score reporting, and NAEP measures academic performance rather than paid tutoring demand. I combine near-flat tutor employment, continued high SAT and ACT participation, renewed score reporting, and persistent learning gaps into a five-year service-demand ratio. Employment may understate demand when productivity rises, while test participation may overstate paid preparation because much testing is school-day and free resources are available.
caveats: Employment projections combine demand, productivity, and delivery-model shifts and cover ten rather than five years.
SAT and ACT takers overlap, and school-day testing does not necessarily generate commercial preparation demand.
NAEP academic need does not establish ability or willingness to pay for private tutoring.
d5.change_evidence: A quarterly five-year panel of 611691 paid sessions, student-hours, active learners, tuition, school contracts, exam-prep share, and churn, deflated for price and adjusted for changes in session quality, linked to SAT, ACT, professional-exam, and K-12 enrollment cohorts.
d5.rationale: BLS projects only 1% tutor employment growth over 2024 to 2034, implying broadly stable underlying demand after productivity. Offsetting pressures are visible: over 2 million class-of-2025 students took the SAT, nearly 1.4 million took the ACT, Common App test-score reporters rose 11% through February 2025, and twelfth-grade reading remained below 2019. Test-optional admissions, household affordability, demographic changes, school-funded program normalization, and AI self-study restrain growth.

caveats: The 2022 NAICS index includes online learning marketplaces as well as direct providers, increasing business-model heterogeneity.
The frozen LMM firm count is itself an estimate and may include firms whose normalized EBITDA falls outside the target band.
Public listings and franchise examples are not representative samples of the full industry.
e.change_evidence: A firm-by-firm review of the 88 dataset-estimated LMM firms covering customer recurrence, revenue by delivery model, owner teaching hours, tutor concentration, franchise and curriculum rights, school-contract renewal, platform status, and change-of-control provisions.
e.rationale: Most LMM firms classified in 611691 should sell repeat external instruction, but not all are transferable under the lens. Exclusions include founder-star tutoring practices, project-funded school contracts, thin online marketplaces, one-exam businesses, and firms whose relationships or curriculum rights cannot survive control transfer. Franchised and multi-instructor centers are more likely to qualify.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 611691 ($3,247,261,762) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 611691 ($8,285,617,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1657 (margins.json[61]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Education EBITDA/Sales 16.57%); revenue/employee $75,859 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 611691); class <5: 4,529 firms, avg revenue/firm $188,081 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $31,165 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 1,726 firms, avg revenue/firm $353,770 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $58,620 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 1,614 firms, avg revenue/firm $539,121 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $89,332 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 990 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,744,792 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $289,112 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 88 firms, avg revenue/firm $17,761,773 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,943,126 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 32 firms, avg revenue/firm $83,208,344 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $13,787,623 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 88

o.bridge: BLS states that online software and generative AI can complete many regular tutor activities, while Stanford measures an AI assistant used by human tutors rather than the share of future industry service still requiring an operator. I treat routine drill, explanation, practice generation, and low-stakes feedback as increasingly self-serviceable, but retain accountable operators for diagnosis, motivation, progress management, parent and school communication, safeguarding, high-stakes strategy, and intervention when students struggle. The estimate may overstate operator need if guarded AI tutors become trusted and outcome-proven, or understate it if parents continue to value human accountability.
caveats: The BLS statement is directional and does not quantify service-volume substitution.
The Stanford trial studied a Southern school district and math tutoring, not consumer LMM operators across subjects.
Rapid improvements in model reliability, multimodal interaction, and verified curriculum alignment could move this range downward.
o.change_evidence: US randomized trials and commercial cohort data comparing AI-only, human-only, and blended tutoring on durable learning, completion, willingness to pay, parent trust, safeguarding incidents, and escalation, with results segmented by age, subject, learning need, and exam stakes.
o.rationale: The operator-required share should decline materially because tutoring is an explicit target for AI substitution and software can provide always-available individualized practice. Yet evidence that AI improves outcomes when it coaches live tutors, plus the importance of relationships, adherence, and escalation, supports a sizable blended-service role rather than near-total software replacement in five years.

caveats: No direct study measures five-year pass-through of AI-driven tutoring savings.
Exam-prep courses, weekly remediation, professional review, and school contracts differ materially in price elasticity.
Volume displacement is excluded here and reflected in demand and operator-required quantities.
q.change_evidence: Anonymized invoices and contracts from at least 20 firms showing revenue by hourly, prepaid, subscription, franchise, and institutional models; renewal and repricing frequency; discounts and refunds; and price or churn responses after documented delivery-cost reductions.
q.rationale: Consumer tutoring is generally priced by session, monthly plan, prepaid package, membership, or fixed exam-prep course, so an operator can retain near-term savings without contractual cost pass-through. Retention erodes quickly because purchases renew frequently, families compare alternatives, tutors can work independently, schools rebid contracts, and AI or free digital practice places a visible ceiling on price.

caveats: The Stanford trial measured learning progress from tutor assistance, not labor removed or avoided in LMM firms.
Implementation may increase service quality or tutor capacity without permitting equivalent payroll reduction.
Professional-exam, special-needs, and K-12 tutoring have different quality and compliance constraints.
rho.change_evidence: Multi-term operating data from franchised and independent centers comparing AI-enabled and control locations on labor hours per student, tutor utilization, escalation and error rates, learning gains, parent satisfaction, refunds, and instructor turnover.
rho.rationale: Within five years, firms can deploy AI in curriculum authoring, targeted practice, tutor coaching, session notes, parent updates, placement support, scheduling, and lead handling. Implementation is constrained by integration, answer reliability, curriculum alignment, student privacy, tutor adoption, parental trust, and the need to prevent answer-giving that weakens learning.

s5.bridge: McKinsey models cross-industry US SMB ownership transitions through 2035, and BizBuySell reports 187 completed 2025 sales across the broad education-and-childcare sector. I convert general retirement pressure and observed education resale activity into a five-year probability, then discount for childcare and school deals outside 611691, sub-LMM transactions, closures, family succession, asset sales, and noncontrol events. The sources may overstate the rate for founder-dependent tutoring firms or understate it because many private deals are unreported.
caveats: No source measures a five-year transfer probability for eligible LMM 611691 firms.
BizBuySell voluntarily reported sales skew small and combine childcare, schools, test preparation, and other education businesses.
McKinsey's modeled retirement transitions include closures and firms across industries and size bands.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of PitchBook, GF Data, Axial, SBA 7(a), franchise-resale, broker, and state ownership records filtered to NAICS 611691 and matched to a known LMM denominator, with each event coded as external control, internal transfer, closure, or asset sale.
s5.rationale: Aging-owner pressure and an established education resale market make control transfers plausible, but a qualifying event remains a minority outcome in five years. Transferability is weakened when client trust and teaching quality reside personally with the owner, while franchises and staffed centers can support a cleaner handoff.

principal_driver: A high labor share is paired with directly automatable practice, feedback, preparation, and administrative workflows that can expand tutor capacity.
principal_weakness: The same AI capability that creates labor savings can eliminate routine paid sessions and rapidly transmit savings to customers through abundant low-cost alternatives.
